18: A direct revelation: 'The word of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God.' Paul's theologia crucis (theology of the cross) is the irreducible Christological claim: God's power is revealed not in imperial might or philosophical sophistry but in the crucified Christ. The cross does not merely illustrate a timeless spiritual principle — it is the specific historical event in which God's wisdom and power are concentrated and disclosed. Every Corinthian pretension to superior wisdom is measured against this standard: does it stand or fall before the cross?
30: A direct revelation: 'He is the source of your life in Christ Jesus, whom God made our wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption.' Christ as the personified Wisdom of God (cf. Proverbs 8:22-31; Sir 24; Wis 1 Corinthians 7:22-8:1) answers Corinthian competition over human wisdom with a Christological absorbing of all wisdom into one person. The four nouns (wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, redemption) are not merely metaphors but ontological claims: in Christ, believers receive these realities as gifts, not achievements. This is the Christological inversion of the Torah: where Torah promised blessing through obedience, Christ provides the reality the Torah pointed toward.
1: The theme: Paul as called apostle of Christ Jesus by God's will. The opening places Christ at the center of the letter's authority structure: Paul's apostleship is a commission from Christ, not a self-appointed role. Every instruction and rebuke in the letter derives its authority from this Christological ground. The NT pattern of divine calling through Christ recapitulates the OT prophet's commissioning: called not by human agency but by divine appointment.
2: The church is defined by two Christological realities: sanctified en Christō Iēsou (in Christ Jesus) and called to be holy people. Holiness in the OT was participation in YHWH's character (Leviticus 11:44-45); in Paul, holiness is participation in Christ's own status before God — the sanctification Christ accomplished is the ground of the community's identity as holy. The letter is addressed to all who call on the name of the Lord — the Shema-style confession now directed to Jesus Christ as Lord.
3: The blessing formula places Christ alongside the Father as the source of grace and peace. The standard Jewish greeting adapted by Paul identifies both God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ as the shared origin of the community's blessing — a Trinitarian structure embedded in every Pauline letter-opening. The co-ordination of Father and Christ as joint source is an implicit Christological claim about Christ's divine status.
4: The grace given in Christ Jesus is the Christological ground of all thanksgiving. The gifts the Corinthians have received are not generic divine endowments but specifically located en Christō Iēsou (in Christ Jesus) — within the sphere of Christ's accomplishment. Gratitude directed toward God for gifts received in Christ creates the anti-boasting logic that will govern the entire letter.
5: Enriched in all speech and knowledge en autō (in him): the gifts of eloquence and understanding are Christ-given, not self-generated achievements. This direct address to the Corinthians' prized capacities establishes them as Christological gifts before critiquing their misuse. Gifts given by Christ cannot be grounds for competition between Christ's people.
6: The testimony of Christ was confirmed among them — Paul's gospel proclamation about Christ was not his own biography but a witness to what Christ had done. The divine confirmation of this testimony (through signs and transformed lives, cf. 1 Corinthians 2:4) is Christ's own authentication of the message about himself. The community's existence is the confirmation of Christ's testimony.
7: The gifts flow from the expectation of Christ's revelation (apokalypsin tou Kyriou Iēsou Christou): the community waits for the unveiling of the Lord Jesus Christ at the Parousia. All spiritual gifts are transitional provisions for the period between resurrection and return — they point toward and will be superseded by Christ's full manifestation. Christological eschatology is the frame within which the letter's discussion of gifts (1 Corinthians 12-14) must be read.
8: Christ will keep them firm to the end so they will be blameless on his Day. The Day of the Lord, now recast as the Day of our Lord Jesus Christ, is the eschatological horizon that gives Paul's ethical instructions their urgency. Blamelessness on that Day is not achieved by human effort but guaranteed by Christ's own faithfulness: he will sustain what he began.
9: The faithfulness of God is expressed through the call into koinōnia with his Son — fellowship, participation, sharing. This is the deepest Christological claim in the letter's opening: the community's life is not merely association with Christ but participation in him. The whole of 1 Corinthians is an argument that this participation in Christ must reshape every sphere of community life: factions, sexuality, food, worship, resurrection hope.
10: The appeal for unity is made through the name of the Lord Jesus Christ — that name carries the authority and identity of Christ himself as the community's single Lord. Division in the community is a fracturing of Christ's own body (1 Corinthians 1:13: is Christ divided?). The unity Paul seeks is not merely social harmony but a Christological necessity: there is one Christ, therefore there must be one community.
11: The report of quarrels (eris) comes from Chloe's household. The factions represent a fundamental misunderstanding of what Christ accomplished: if different apostles are merely channels through whom one Christ operates, there is no basis for choosing between them. The quarrels reveal a misplaced Christology — treating apostolic personalities as the ultimate referent instead of Christ himself.
12: The party-names (I follow Paul; I follow Apollos; I follow Cephas; I follow Christ) all reveal the same error: making Christ one option among several allegiances. Even the Christ-party, if it understood itself as superior to the others, was using Christ as a faction-banner rather than recognizing him as the Lord of all the parties. The use of Christ's name to build a competitive faction is a misappropriation of his person.
13: Three rhetorical questions expose the absurdity of the factions: Is Christ divided? — No, his body is one. Was Paul crucified for you? — No, only Christ was crucified for sinners. Were you baptized into Paul's name? — No, baptism creates union with Christ, not with apostles. All three questions place the saving events — cross and baptism — in Christ alone, not in any apostolic figure. Christology demolishes the factional logic.
14: Paul's relief at not having baptized many in Corinth is grounded in the exclusive Christological allegiance that baptism creates. Baptism in the NT is eis Christon (into Christ), not into an apostle's sphere of influence. The factional problem is partly liturgical: baptism wrongly understood creates patron-client bonds with the baptizer rather than covenant-union with Christ.
15: The concern is that no one could say they were baptized into Paul's name — the name of baptism belongs to Christ alone. The threefold baptismal name (Matthew 28:19: Father, Son, Holy Spirit) or the simple name of the Lord Jesus Christ (Acts 2:38) establishes Christ as the one into whose reality believers enter at baptism. No apostle can share that exclusive position.
16: The parenthetical admission about Stephanas's household does not undermine the point: Paul is not denying that he baptized, but that he baptized in his own name or to create personal followings. Christological allegiance through baptism is to Christ; the agent of baptism is irrelevant to the union created.
17: The cross must not be emptied (kenōthē ho stauros) by the wisdom of words: rhetorical display draws attention to the speaker and away from the saving event. The cross is the singular Christological fact; everything else serves to make it visible or obscures it. The tension between eloquent proclamation and cross-centred content runs through all Pauline preaching.
19: Isaiah 29:14 — God will destroy the wisdom of the wise — is read as prophetic anticipation of the cross. The same divine logic that judged Israel's trust in human wisdom now operates through the crucified Christ: God's wisdom-demolishing wisdom is concentrated in the foolishness of the cross. The prophetic judgment on human self-reliance finds its ultimate expression in the cross event.
20: The cross has made obsolete the categories of present-age wisdom: the philosopher, the scribe, the rhetor have no answer to the cross. Christ crucified is not a proposition that can be evaluated by the world's standards because it belongs to the coming age breaking into the present — the age whose wisdom the cross inaugurates. The cross renders the wise people of this age (ho aion houtos) without a valid framework.
21: God chose to save through the foolishness of proclamation: the proclaimed Christ-event is the vehicle of salvation that natural wisdom cannot replicate. The wisdom of God deployed in the cross is not a higher grade of the same rational process but a completely different epistemological mode — revelation through historical event and Spirit-enabled reception, not philosophical demonstration. Christology is the content of the foolish proclamation that saves.
22: Jews seek signs and Greeks seek wisdom: both are seeking validation in their own preferred categories. Christ crucified satisfies neither demand on its own terms — he is not the sign of military victory Jews hoped for, nor the philosophical sage Greeks admired. He is something categorically different: the wisdom and power of God in the form of a crucified criminal, legible only from within the community shaped by the Spirit.
23: We preach Christ crucified: Paul's singular content. A crucified Messiah was a contradiction in Jewish expectation (Messiah = triumphant Davidic king) and a scandal in Greek thought (the divine cannot suffer shame). The cross is precisely this offense — it refuses to be domesticated into either tradition's pre-existing categories. This is why Christ crucified requires new categories: the cross creates the epistemological conditions for understanding it.
24: Christ as the power of God and the wisdom of God: the two titles absorb what Jews and Greeks respectively sought. They were right to seek power and wisdom — they were wrong about their sources and forms. Christ satisfies both quests at the level of ultimate fulfillment: God's own power (not Roman imperial power) and God's own wisdom (not Sophistic eloquence) are both incarnated and crucified in Christ.
25: The foolishness of God is wiser than human wisdom; the weakness of God is stronger than human strength: the paradox is not resolved but intensified. This is not saying that the cross turns out to be secretly strong after all by human standards — it remains genuinely weak by those standards. The point is that divine categories transcend human ones: God's apparent foolishness and weakness exceed all human maxima. Christ crucified is not a halfway-house between wisdom and folly but an entirely different axis.
26: Not many wise, powerful, or noble were called: the social composition of the community is itself a Christological statement. God's election operates through the cross-pattern — choosing what the world despises. The same wisdom that chose the cross chose the weak and lowly as the primary recipients. The community's social profile is an embodied argument for the cross's logic.
27: God chose the foolish things to shame the wise, the weak things to shame the strong. This divine reversal pattern runs from the OT through the beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12) and the Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55) and finds its concentrated expression in the cross: the King of Israel dies as a criminal, the strong one is crucified in weakness. The community's existence as the foolish-and-weak-chosen continues the cross-pattern into the social world.
28: The lowly, despised, and non-existent things chosen by God to nullify things that are: the cross is God's act of new creation from nothing. As God created from non-being in Genesis 1:1, so in the cross God creates new covenant-people from those who were nobodies. The eschatological community is the creation-from-nothing that the cross enacts — the ultimate demonstration that salvation is entirely God's act, not human achievement.
29: So that no flesh may boast before God: the anti-boasting purpose of divine election through weakness and the cross. Boasting (kauchēsis) in Paul is always displaced by the cross: anyone who genuinely encounters the crucified Christ has no standing to claim achievement-credit. The cross does not merely lower human pride — it eliminates the ontological ground for it.
31: Let the one who boasts boast in the Lord (Jeremiah 9:24): the only legitimate boast is one directed toward God — specifically, knowing God through Christ. In the NT context, boasting in the Lord means boasting in the crucified and risen Christ, in whom all divine gifts are given. Christological doxology is the redeemed form of human pride: glory directed entirely toward the God revealed in Christ.
